---
title: "Non-State Armed Groups as Signals in Autocracies"
subtitle: "Ideological Commitment and Opposition Resolve"
author: "Tom Hanna"
affiliation: "University of Houston, Department of Political Science"
date: today
bibliography: 
  - references.bib
  - data_sources.bib
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    fig-cap-location: top
    tbl-cap-location: top
  pdf:
    documentclass: report
    classoption: [a4paper, 12pt]
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    fig-cap-location: top
    tbl-cap-location: top
---
## Overview

This manuscript presents two related papers that examine how autocratic leaders use support for foreign non-state armed groups (NAGs) as costly signals in domestic politics.

The first paper demonstrates that leaders who rely on ideologically committed support coalitions provide visible support to foreign NAGs to signal ideological consistency to their core backers. This support functions as a non-material payoff that strengthens regime stability without requiring constant material redistribution.

The second paper shows that the same visible support for foreign NAGs also serves as a signal of resolve directed at domestic opposition groups. Leaders facing larger or more organized opposition increase NAG support strategically, yet they carefully avoid backing groups that target democracies in order to limit international retaliation.

Both papers draw on the same underlying data infrastructure built from the GRAVE-D dataset and related sources. They share a common theoretical framework centered on two-level signaling in authoritarian politics, while testing distinct audience mechanisms (supporters versus opposition).

These analyses extend the earlier project on autocratic conflict initiation and ideological signaling (Hanna, autocracy_conflict_signaling repository). Where the prior work focused on militarized interstate disputes as signals, the current papers shift attention to lower-intensity, deniable conflict export through non-state armed groups. Together, the three papers illustrate a broader pattern: rational autocrats deploy international behavior as a tool to manage domestic coalition dynamics.

## Manuscript Structure

- **Introduction** — Presents the dual signaling puzzles and links the two papers.
- **Theory** — Develops the common theoretical framework of ideological and resolve signaling.
- **Data and Methods** — Describes the GRAVE-D-based dataset, variable construction, and modeling approach.
- **Paper 2: Ideological Commitment Signaling** — Empirical tests of signaling to core support groups.
- **Paper 3: Opposition Resolve Signaling** — Empirical tests of signaling to domestic opponents.
- **Conclusion** — Summarizes findings, compares the two mechanisms, and discusses implications.
- **Appendix** — Contains additional robustness checks, full descriptive statistics, and extended tables.

The empirical sections draw directly on tables and figures generated by the R scripts in the `R/` directory and saved to `results/tables/` and `results/plots/`. Descriptive statistics, correlation matrices, and corrplots appear in the Data and Methods chapter (with fuller versions in the Appendix).

## How to Navigate This Document

Readers interested primarily in the theoretical contribution should begin with the Theory chapter. Those focused on empirical results can move directly to the two paper-specific results chapters. The shared Introduction and Data and Methods sections provide the necessary background for both.

This work is part of a larger research program on authoritarian leadership politics and international behavior. Earlier stages of the project are available in the companion repository [`autocracy_conflict_signaling`](https://github.com/tomhanna-uh/autocracy_conflict_signaling).

```{r}
#| label: setup-index
#| include: false

# Any global setup needed for the index page (rarely required)
library(knitr)
library(tidyverse)
```
